summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide for micro-ISV owners using e-junkie as their payment processor to hide the e-junkie branding from customers. He covers three specific issues: redirecting customers to your own thank-you page after checkout, fixing email deliverability problems caused by SPF record mismatches, and using DNS CNAME records to mask e-junkie URLs. The post reflects his philosophy that professional branding matters even for small software businesses. He emphasizes security considerations when implementing URL-based parameter passing and notes that these small details affect conversion rates and customer trust.
key_points: Redirect post-checkout pages to your own domain to display CD keys and instructions, rather than showing customers an e-junkie branded page
Publish SPF records so e-junkie's servers are authorized to send email on your behalf, preventing spam filters from rejecting purchase confirmations
Use DNS CNAME records to alias your own subdomain to e-junkie, hiding third-party branding from the URL bar
Customers don't read post-purchase pages unless given obvious reasons to, so make key information prominent to reduce support costs
money_quotes: I really, really can't stand the name — it says absolutely nothing positive to my customers about my business.
Make it big and bold because people do not read this page unless you give them a darn obvious reason to. Getting them to read it saves you support costs, trust me.
Either way, please secure your website against ye-olde insert-arbitrary-content-by-rewriting-the-URL trick. You'll thank me later.
Bad news bears, this means that many automated checks on that email will say 'e-junkie.com is spoofing honestmicroisv.com, oh no, its spaaaaam!'
You aren't using the Fat Free Cart? OK, fix that right now. And watch your conversion rise!
key_insight: Small software businesses should invest effort in hiding third-party service branding from their checkout flow, because professional presentation directly affects customer trust, email deliverability, and conversion rates.
